sentences.base_language: Tomás wakes up and doesn't feel well at all.
sentences.target_language: Tomás se despierta y no se siente nada bien.
sentences.grammarNote:
**se despierta** — **despertarse** (to wake up), reflexive e→ie stem-changer: me despierto, te despiertas, **se despierta**.
**no se siente nada bien** — **nada** here intensifies the negation: 'not at all well' (literally 'nothing well').
**sentirse** (to feel) is reflexive e→ie: me siento, te sientes, **se siente**. Used for physical/emotional states.

sentences.base_language: His throat hurts and his head is pounding.
sentences.target_language: Le duele la garganta y tiene un dolor de cabeza terrible.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Le duele la garganta** — **doler** works like gustar. The throat (subject) 'pains him' (indirect object le).
For multiple aches: le duelen los huesos (his bones ache). Match verb number to the body part, not the person.

sentences.base_language: He takes his temperature. He has a slight fever — 37.8 degrees.
sentences.target_language: Se toma la temperatura. Tiene algo de fiebre: treinta y siete con ocho.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Se toma la temperatura** — reflexive **tomarse** with body-related actions; **la temperatura** uses the definite article.
Spanish decimal notation: **treinta y siete con ocho** (37.8) — **con** replaces the decimal point in spoken Spanish.
**algo de fiebre** (a slight/some fever) — **algo de** + noun = 'a bit of': algo de fiebre, algo de hambre, algo de frío.

sentences.base_language: His roommate Camila comes in. 'Oh no, you're sick!'
sentences.target_language: Su compañera Camila entra. —¡Ay no, estás enfermo!
sentences.grammarNote:
**estás enfermo** — **estar** (not ser) is used with **enfermo** because illness is a temporary state.
Contrast: **ser enfermizo** (to be sickly — a permanent trait) vs. **estar enfermo** (to be sick right now).
**—** (em dash) marks dialogue in Spanish — used instead of quotation marks to introduce direct speech.

sentences.base_language: 'I think I caught a cold. I'm so congested and I keep sneezing.'
sentences.target_language: —Creo que agarré un resfriado. Estoy muy congestionado y no paro de estornudar.
sentences.grammarNote:
**No parar de + infinitive** = to not stop doing something:
no paro de estornudar (I can't stop sneezing), no para de llover (it won't stop raining)

sentences.base_language: 'You need to rest and drink lots of fluids.'
sentences.grammarNote:
**necesitar + infinitive** (to need to do something) — **necesitas** is the tú form.
**tomar** here means 'to drink/consume' (liquids, medicine) — very versatile verb: tomar agua, tomar medicamento, tomar el sol (to sunbathe), tomar una decisión.
**muchos líquidos** — **mucho/a** agrees in gender and number with the noun: mucho agua, muchos líquidos, muchas frutas.

sentences.base_language: 'I know. Can you make me some tea with honey? Please?'
sentences.target_language: —Ya sé. ¿Me puedes hacer un té con miel? ¿Por favor?
sentences.grammarNote:
**Ya sé** (I know / I already know) — **ya** adds a sense of 'already' or mild impatience here.
**¿Me puedes hacer...?** — indirect object **me** (for me) + **poder + infinitive**: 'can you make me...?'
Alternative word order: **¿Puedes hacerme un té?** — the indirect object pronoun can attach to the infinitive or go before the conjugated verb.

sentences.base_language: 'Of course! You should also take some medicine to bring the fever down.'
sentences.target_language: —¡Claro que sí! También deberías tomar algo para bajar la fiebre.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Deberías** (you should) is the conditional tense of deber — softer and more polite than **debes** (you must).
Use deberías for friendly advice: deberías descansar (you should rest), deberías ir al médico (you should see a doctor).

sentences.base_language: Camila makes him chamomile tea and brings him a blanket.
sentences.target_language: Camila le hace un té de manzanilla y le trae una manta.
sentences.grammarNote:
**le hace** and **le trae** — indirect object pronoun **le** (to him) repeated before each verb.
In Spanish, the indirect object pronoun is repeated even when the recipient is already clear: **Camila le hace [a él] un té** — **le** can't be dropped.
**de manzanilla** (chamomile) — **de** introduces the type/flavor: té de manzanilla, té de menta, té de jengibre.

sentences.base_language: 'Stay in bed today. I'll bring you soup later.'
sentences.target_language: —Quédate en cama hoy. Más tarde te traigo sopa.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Quédate** — reflexive informal (tú) command of **quedarse** (to stay/remain): **quédate** (stay!).
Affirmative reflexive commands: the pronoun attaches to the end of the verb: **quédate, levántate, siéntate** (stay, get up, sit down).
**Más tarde te traigo sopa** — present tense used for a near-future promise: 'I'll bring you soup later' (lit. 'later I bring you soup'). Very natural in spoken Spanish.

sentences.base_language: Tomás wraps himself in the blanket. 'Thank you, Camila. I hope I feel better tomorrow.'
sentences.target_language: Tomás se envuelve en la manta. —Gracias, Camila. Ojalá mañana esté mejor.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Ojalá** (I hope / hopefully) always triggers the subjunctive — from Arabic 'inshallah.'
Ojalá + present subjunctive for present/future hopes: Ojalá esté mejor (I hope he's better).
For very unlikely wishes: ojalá + imperfect subjunctive: Ojalá tuviera más tiempo.
